Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic this semester. I'm a hands-on learner, so I tried to do practice problems and work through things physically rather than just reading. I'd usually set aside blocks of time on weekends to catch up, maybe 16 hours across the week, but it never felt like enough with 17 courses to juggle. I kept telling myself I'd get more organized, but life kept getting in the way.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what was due and how overwhelmed I felt. Some weeks I'd be really consistent, studying every evening, but then stress would hit and I'd fall off track completely. Deadlines definitely drove when I studied most intensely. I tried to be regular, but with everything going on, regularity felt like a luxury I couldn't afford.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline looming or getting a notification that something new was posted. Sometimes my peer study group would message me asking about an assignment, and that would prompt me to log in and check what I'd missed. Honestly, guilt was a big motivator too—I'd see how far behind I was and force myself to at least look at the materials.
Q4: There was a brief period mid-semester where logging in became more automatic, maybe after I connected with some classmates through the peer support program. Having people to check in with made me feel accountable. But that routine fell apart when my stress levels spiked and I started missing classes more. The habit just couldn't stick when everything else was falling apart.
Q5: The video lectures were genuinely helpful because I could pause and rewind when something didn't click. I watched almost 300 minutes of videos because that format worked better for my learning style than just reading text. Discussion posts were useful too when peers explained things in different ways—sometimes their explanations made more sense than the official materials.
Q6: I often ignored the reading materials and some of the supplementary resources. They felt too passive for how I learn best. I'd tell myself I'd get to them later, but later never came. The discussion forums I only used minimally because by the time I logged in, conversations had already moved on and I felt too behind to contribute meaningfully.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see immediate application or when it would directly help my grade. Practice problems and anything hands-on felt valuable because I could actually do something, not just absorb information. If I couldn't see how something connected to the exam or assignments, it was hard to prioritize it over everything else demanding my attention.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a trap, honestly. I knew they might help, but with my low attendance and all the required stuff I was already behind on, optional felt like a luxury. Required activities had urgency attached to them, even if I didn't always complete them on time. Optional stuff just kept getting pushed to a 'someday' list that never came.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded a lot of materials and worked through them on my tablet during commutes or when I didn't have good internet. I also used external YouTube videos and practice problem sets I found online that explained concepts differently. My peer group shared notes and resources through WhatsApp that never touched the LMS at all.
Q10: There were many times I'd be working through downloaded problem sets for hours, writing things out by hand, really engaging with the material, but none of that showed up anywhere. I'd also meet with my study group at the library and we'd work through concepts together on whiteboards. That was some of my most productive learning, but it was completely invisible online.
Q11: Yes, constantly. I'd download lecture videos or PDFs once and then work with them offline for days. My assignment scores were actually decent—74 percent—because I was putting in work, just not always in ways the system could track. I preferred having materials saved locally so I could annotate them and access them without worrying about connectivity.
Q12: Around midterms, everything got harder. My stress was through the roof, I was feeling really sad and overwhelmed, and my attendance dropped significantly. I was dealing with some personal stuff that made it hard to even get out of bed some days, let alone engage with coursework. The peer support helped a bit, but it wasn't enough to pull me back on track.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted or stressed, I'd log into the LMS but just stare at the screen without really processing anything. Competing responsibilities—work, family stuff, other courses—meant the LMS often got the leftover scraps of my energy. Confusion about what was actually required versus optional also made me waste time figuring out priorities instead of actually studying.
Q14: Many times. I'd open videos and let them play while my mind wandered somewhere else entirely. Or I'd scroll through discussion posts without really reading them, just going through the motions. It looked like engagement on paper, but mentally I was checked out. High stress does that to me—I'm present physically but absent mentally.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even knowing they were important. The anxiety made starting feel impossible sometimes—like if I couldn't do it perfectly, why start at all? I'd tell myself I'd feel more ready tomorrow, but tomorrow I'd feel the same way. It's a cycle I recognize but struggle to break, especially when I'm already feeling low.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to submit work. I'd have bursts of intense activity right before something was due, cramming and rushing. My LMS logins probably spiked dramatically around deadline periods. Without that external pressure, I'd drift and procrastinate. It wasn't healthy, but it was how I survived.
Q17: When I got feedback, especially positive feedback, it motivated me to try harder on the next thing. But feedback often came too late to help me adjust, or I was already so behind that it felt discouraging rather than helpful. The peer support feedback was actually more impactful because it felt more personal and immediate than official course feedback.
Q18: The course structure felt overwhelming with so many modules and resources. I never quite knew what to prioritize, which made me anxious and led to avoidance. A clearer, more linear structure would have helped me stay on track. The flexibility that was probably meant to help actually made it easier for me to fall behind without realizing how far gone I was.
Q19: Having so many online courses made it hard to keep track of everything across different platforms and schedules. The discussion forums were helpful when they were active, but intimidating when I felt too behind to participate. Video content was easier to engage with than text-heavy materials. More structured check-ins might have caught me before I fell so far behind.
Q20: I think that describes me somewhat. I was logging in, watching videos, browsing materials, but my actual submission rate was terrible. For me, it was anxiety and perfectionism—I'd engage with content but freeze when it came to producing something to be graded. The gap between consuming and creating felt enormous when I was stressed.
Q21: That makes sense to me because so much real learning happens offline. Someone could download everything, study with friends, use external resources, and barely touch the LMS while still understanding the material well. The LMS only captures one narrow slice of how people actually learn. I wish my offline efforts had translated better to my final grade.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when you're active, what you click on, how long you spend. But they miss the quality of that engagement entirely. They can't tell if you're really learning or just going through motions. They miss offline study, group work, the emotional state behind the clicks. Numbers without context can be really misleading.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks. It's when I'm curious, asking questions, connecting ideas to real life. For me, engagement is hands-on—doing problems, discussing with peers, applying concepts. It's not about time spent logged in; it's about mental investment and genuine effort to learn.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how hard I was fighting to stay afloat despite everything. It wouldn't show the peer support sessions that kept me going, the offline study groups, the hours spent with downloaded materials. It wouldn't show my sadness or stress levels, or how much I actually cared even when my numbers looked bad. There's a whole person behind those disappointing statistics.
